steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The passage establishes that King's Cross suggests to Margaret "infinity" and "eternal adventure" — values explicitly opposed to materialism and capitalist London. The correct choice must reflect that idealist, non-material significance without misreading the station as a symbol of capitalism or disillusionment.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — As a point of connection between London and the countryside, King's Cross suggests to Margaret the possibility of acquiring the intangible abundance promised by the kinds of authentic engagements that the novel's rural spaces seem to offer.: frames King's Cross as a gateway to the countryside's non-material values — infinity, authentic engagement — directly matching the passage's idealist reading. ✓
  • Choice B — Because it is situated at the beginning of Margaret's journey from the city to the country, King's Cross emblematizes the intrusion of the forces of materialism and modernity into the rural spaces that the novel associates with idealism and tradition.: inverts the passage's logic. King's Cross is not associated with materialism; it points away from London's capitalism toward the countryside. ✗
  • Choice C — The austerity conveyed by King's Cross's appearance mirrors Margaret's disillusionment with the prospect of having authentic connections with other people in a world that chiefly values more conventional forms of prosperity.: introduces disillusionment, which contradicts "infinity" and "eternal adventure" — the station inspires wonder, not disappointment. ✗
  • Choice D — King's Cross has a relatively unassuming appearance whose sharp contrast with the more aesthetically pleasing appearance of St. Pancras suggests to Margaret the ascendancy of the pragmatic capitalistic outlook among London's inhabitants.: neither the passage's comparison with St. Pancras nor capitalism as Margaret's takeaway is supported — this misreads the aesthetic plainness as a capitalist signal. ✗
That matches Choice A — As a point of connection between London and the countryside, King's Cross suggests to Margaret the possibility of acquiring the intangible abundance promised by the kinds of authentic engagements that the novel's rural spaces seem to offer..
